g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore isAlive() and join() methods for thread synchronization in Java multithreading.
Multithreading allows multiple threads to run concurrently, improving program performance and responsiveness.
g.text: A programming technique where multiple threads execute concurrently to improve
performance and responsiveness. Each thread is a lightweight subprocess.

g.data-narration: When threads run together, we need synchronization to ensure orderly execution and data consistency.
g.text: Main thread must sometimes wait for child thread to finish before continuing.
Example: Exit program only after all tasks complete. Two methods: isAlive() and join()

g.data-narration: The isAlive() method checks if a thread is still running. Returns true between start() and run() completion.
g.text: Purpose: Checks if a thread is currently executing
Returns true: After start() is called but before run() completes
Returns false: Before start() is called OR after run() completes

g.data-narration: Here's how isAlive() works in practice - polling in a loop to check if child thread has finished.
The isAlive() polling approach wastes CPU cycles by continuously checking - not recommended for production.
g.text: Polling: Continuously checking isAlive() in a loop wastes CPU cycles!
CPU Waste: While main thread busy-loops, CPU could be doing useful work elsewhere
Recommendation: Use join() instead - it's the efficient, standard approach!

g.data-narration: The join() method blocks the calling thread until the target thread terminates - efficient and recommended!
g.text: Purpose: Blocks the calling thread until the target thread completes execution
Behavior: Main thread waits passively - no CPU waste, efficient blocking
Key: Must wrap in try-catch for InterruptedException

g.data-narration: Clean and simple - just call join() after start(). The try-catch handles interruption gracefully.
Key difference: isAlive uses polling (wastes CPU), join uses blocking (efficient). Always prefer join()!
Both methods have trade-offs. join() is simpler and efficient but blocks the thread. Choose based on your needs.
Remember these exam tips: join() is preferred, wrap in try-catch, and understand when isAlive returns true/false.
g.text: • isAlive() returns false BEFORE start() and AFTER completion
• join() is the STANDARD way to wait for thread completion in exams
• Always wrap join() and sleep() in try-catch for InterruptedException

g.data-narration: To summarize: use join() for thread waiting - it's efficient, simple, and the recommended approach for exams.
g.text: isAlive(): Polling method - checks thread state, wastes CPU (not recommended)
join(): Blocking method - waits for thread completion, efficient and standard (recommended!)
🎯 Exam Answer: "Use join() instead of isAlive() + loop - it doesn't waste CPU cycles!"
